The branch adds declared plugin and MCP allowlist context to the shared tool-policy pipeline across agent, gateway, embedded-runner, and skill surfaces, with availability and denylist regression coverage.

PR surface: Source +292, Tests +337. Total +629 across 9 files.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows current main only classifies allowlists against materialized plugin groups and core tools, while the PR adds declared context and focused tests for valid MCP namespaces, typos, disabled entries, exact denies, broad wildcard denies, and duplicate-safe prefixes.

target.title: fix(policy): recognize declared tool allowlists
target.body:
## Summary
- Recognize declared tool allowlists while filtering unavailable tools out of the declared context.
- Filter denied MCP namespaces from declared allowlists, including `bundle-mcp`, sanitized server prefixes such as `paperless__*`, duplicate-safe suffixed prefixes such as `vigil-harbor-2__*`, broader wildcard denies such as `paperless*`, and exact tool denies such as `paperless__delete`.
- Keep this scoped to runtime policy diagnostics only; no config schema/options, CLI flags, plugin API/contract, env vars, migrations, or runtime tool filtering surface changes.

## Validation
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tool-policy-pipeline.test.ts src/agents/tool-policy.plugin-only-allowlist.test.ts src/plugins/tools.optional.test.ts src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-tools.request-boundary.test.ts --reporter=dot` (3 shards, 114 tests)
- `node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/tool-policy-declared-context.ts src/agents/tool-policy-pipeline.test.ts src/skills/runtime/tool-dispatch.ts src/agents/agent-tools.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/effective-tool-policy.ts src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts`
- `node_modules/oxlint/bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/agents/tool-policy-declared-context.ts src/agents/tool-policy-pipeline.test.ts`
- `node_modules/oxlint/bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/skills/runtime/tool-dispatch.ts src/agents/agent-tools.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/effective-tool-policy.ts src/gateway/tool-resolution.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real Behavior Proof
- GitHub `Real behavior proof` workflow passed on prior head `0342803885c57a8c7cfe1a6aa8ec486b2d5330a7`: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/27592084887
- Current head `55d6738b585a036b29a5e864f013f5d6e4de1d93` is pushed; GitHub checks are being refreshed.

## Current Head
- Rebased on current `main`.
- Signed head: `55d6738b585a036b29a5e864f013f5d6e4de1d93`.

target.title: fix(mattermost): preserve text-block boundaries in draft preview (#87322)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #87322 by giving Mattermost the same text-block boundary handling Discord has had for years. Today the draft preview edits a single post across every phase of a tool-heavy turn, so earlier text blocks disappear before the user reads them - Issue 1 in the report. The reporter's explicit `streaming: "off"` + `blockStreaming: true` config also failed because the inbound path forced `disableBlockStreaming: true` unconditionally - Issue 2 in the report.

This PR keeps the existing canonical `streaming.mode: "off" | "partial" | "block" | "progress"` contract on main (`src/config/types.base.ts:12`) - no schema change, no new config keys.

## Behavior change

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` - inbound dispatch path (`monitorMattermostProvider`):

- **Block-reply path now honored when preview is off.** Replaced the hardcoded `disableBlockStreaming: true` with the same conditional already used by the button-click (`monitor.ts:832`) and slash-command (`monitor.ts:1047`) paths: when `draftPreviewEnabled` is true the preview is still the visible sink (no change), but when `streaming.mode === "off"` the path now honors the operator's explicit `account.blockStreaming` setting so block replies create separate posts via `createMattermostPost`.
- **Preview rotates at text-block boundaries when `streaming.mode === "block"`.** New `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` in `draft-stream.ts` holds a "did we just stream something?" gate. At `onAssistantMessageStart`, `onReasoningEnd`, and `onToolStart` the controller calls `draftStream.forceNewMessage()` (the primitive already on the stream - `draft-stream.ts:133`) if and only if a draft update was emitted since the last boundary. The next `draftStream.update(...)` then runs the `else` arm of `sendOrEditStreamMessage` (`draft-stream.ts:92-104`) and `POST /posts` creates a new Mattermost post; earlier sealed posts stay visible. The gate is the same shape Discord uses (`extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.draft-preview.ts:132-138`) - back-to-back boundaries without content never emit empty preview posts.
- **All other modes unchanged.** `streaming.mode === "partial"` (default) keeps the edit-in-place feel. `"progress"` keeps its single rolling progress post. `"off"` keeps its no-preview behavior.

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts` - added `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController({ enabled, forceNewMessage })`. Local helper, no exposure through `api.ts`.

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts` - added unit tests for the controller (enabled/disabled, content gate, back-to-back boundaries) and for `draftStream.forceNewMessage()` itself (next update creates a new `POST /posts` call).

`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts` - extended the existing `vi.mock("./draft-stream.js", …)` shim with a no-op `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` stub so the suite keeps wiring through the mocked draft module.

## Compatibility and exported surface (maintainer sign-off requested)

Two intentional changes need explicit maintainer acceptance before merge:

- **Visible delivery-shape change.** For `streaming.mode: "block"` and for preview-off `blockStreaming: true`, a tool-heavy turn now produces multiple visible Mattermost posts (one per text/tool boundary) instead of one repeatedly-edited post. This is the fix for #87322 (earlier blocks were being overwritten), but it is a user-visible change for operators already running those configurations. Default `streaming.mode: "partial"` and `"progress"` are unchanged, and `"off"` keeps its no-preview behavior.
- **Exported draft-loop surface.** This adds an optional `takePending?(): string` member to the exported `DraftStreamLoop` type (`src/plugin-sdk/channel-outbound`). It is additive and optional, so existing plugin-SDK implementors are not broken, but it is a plugin-SDK-adjacent surface change that maintainers should accept.

I cannot accept these on the maintainers' behalf; flagging them here so the delivery-shape and exported-type changes are an explicit pre-merge decision.

## Diff size

```
 docs/channels/mattermost.md                        |   6 +-
 docs/concepts/progress-drafts.md                   |  16 +-
 docs/concepts/streaming.md                         |   5 +-
 .../mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts | 271 ++++++++++++++++++++-
 .../mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts      |  76 ++++++-
 .../monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts           | 111 ++++++++++-
 extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts    |  34 ++-
 src/channels/draft-stream-loop.ts                  |  10 +
 8 files changed, 502 insertions(+), 27 deletions(-)
```

## Boundary flush (addresses ClawSweeper P1 finding)

ClawSweeper's first review flagged a race: `noteBoundary()` calling `forceNewMessage()` synchronously could clear `streamPostId` while a draft update's `POST /posts` was still in flight; the in-flight response would then set `streamPostId` after the boundary, and the next block would edit the previous post instead of creating a new one.

`forceNewMessage` is now async and awaits `loop.flush()` before clearing `streamPostId` and resetting pending state. The controller's `noteBoundary` is async too; the inbound-dispatch callbacks (`onAssistantMessageStart`, `onReasoningEnd`, `onToolStart`) await it before resetting `lastPartialText`. A new `draft-stream.test.ts` regression case (`flushes a pending in-flight create before forcing a new post`) holds the first `POST /posts` response promise, fires the boundary while the create is still in flight, then asserts the second block lands as a separate `POST /posts` (not a `PUT /posts/{id}` edit of the first post). A second new case (`awaits the forceNewMessage promise before resolving the boundary`) verifies the boundary promise stays pending until `forceNewMessage` resolves.

## Pending-partial boundary seal (addresses ClawSweeper re-review P1 at `draft-stream.ts:167-168`)

ClawSweeper's latest re-review flagged a deeper boundary race. When a block's first `POST /posts` create is still in flight and a later cumulative `onPartialReply` leaves text pending for the same block, `forceNewMessage` bumped `postGeneration` and cleared `streamPostId` before awaiting the in-flight create. When the create then resolved, its `gen === postGeneration` guard failed because the generation had already advanced, so the returned post id was discarded. `sealedPostId` had already been captured as `undefined`, so the pending text went out as a second `POST /posts` instead of editing the just-created post. A slow server left the user with both a stale partial post and the sealed block post.

Fix (`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.ts`): `forceNewMessage` takes the pending seal text first (cancelling the throttle timer), then synchronously bumps `postGeneration` and clears the live `streamPostId` before awaiting the in-flight create. Each create now records its post id keyed by the generation it was issued under (`createdPostIdsByGeneration`), so after the await the boundary recovers the finished block's real post id from that record and edits it with the pending cumulative text instead of creating a duplicate. Clearing the cursor before the await (rather than after) is what also makes the detach synchronous; see the next section for why that ordering is load-bearing under fire-and-forget callbacks.

Regression test (`draft-stream.test.ts`, `seals a pending partial onto the in-flight created post instead of duplicating it`): queues a second cumulative partial while the first create is held in flight, then fires the boundary and asserts the request sequence is `POST /posts` then `PUT /posts/post-1` (one create, one in-place edit). The previous ordering produced `['/posts', '/posts']` (two creates, the duplicate ClawSweeper flagged); the fix produces `['/posts', '/posts/post-1']`. The generation-keyed record is what lets the seal still find the just-created post id even though the generation is bumped before the await.

Block-mode tool-progress test correction (`monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts`): the `keeps consecutive tool-progress updates on one post in block streaming` case now drives three `bash` progress events with distinct commands and `detailMode: "raw"`. Bare same-tool progress with hidden detail renders to an identical preview line, and the progress compositor correctly dedups identical text to a single update, so the prior version asserted three preview updates against one rendered line. With distinct rendered lines the case now exercises its real intent: three in-place updates on one post, no boundary for same-tool progress, and exactly one boundary when a new tool starts.

## Synchronous boundary detach (addresses ClawSweeper re-review P1 at `draft-stream.ts:165-168`)

ClawSweeper's last re-review made the load-bearing point that the embedded-agent subscriber does not await the boundary callback. `onPartialReply`/`onReasoningStream` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.ts:281,1193`) and `onAssistantMessageStart`/`onReasoningEnd` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.messages.ts:144,573,687`) are all dispatched with `void`. So the boundary callback's async tail is not sequenced before the next partial. With the previous ordering, `forceNewMessage` awaited the in-flight op *before* clearing `streamPostId`, which meant the live post id stayed attached to the finished block across the await. A partial for the next block that arrived during that await - flushing immediately once the throttle window was open - would read the still-attached `streamPostId` and `PUT` its text onto the previous block's post, overwriting it. Whether the overwrite happened depended on microtask ordering between the boundary's clear and the partial's flush, which is exactly the kind of "incorrect under the real caller" the finding called out.

Fix: `forceNewMessage` now bumps the generation and clears `streamPostId` synchronously, before it awaits anything. The instant the boundary fires, the live cursor is detached, so any fire-and-forget partial that arrives next deterministically opens a fresh post regardless of microtask ordering. The seal onto the previous block's in-flight create is preserved via the generation-keyed record described above. This is the "detach synchronously" option from the review.

Real behavior proof (live HTTP, fire-and-forget interleave): a loopback Mattermost v4 server (`POST /api/v4/posts` artificially held 120ms) driving the production `createMattermostDraftStream` over real `fetch` round-trips. Block #1 creates `post-1`; the throttle window is allowed to elapse; the boundary is then fired fire-and-forget (`void forceNewMessage()`, not awaited, as the embedded subscriber does), and block #2's partial arrives next. The same script was run against the committed pre-fix source (`56186cfe25`) and the fix.

BEFORE (committed `56186cfe25`):

```
AGENT      -> text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now...')  [creates first post]
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Looking into the logs now..."
   stream.postId() after block #1 = "post-1"
   (300ms elapsed: throttle window open, so the next update flushes immediately)
AGENT      -> onToolStart [BOUNDARY] fired fire-and-forget (void), NOT awaited
   stream.postId() immediately after boundary call = "post-1"
AGENT      -> text block #2 ('Found the bad commit. Reverting now.') arrives before boundary settles
MM SERVER  <- PUT  /posts/post-1  message="Found the bad commit. Reverting now."
===== RESULT =====
POST /posts (creates): 1  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 1
block #1 post (post-1) final visible message = "Found the bad commit. Reverting now."
=> block #1 post was OVERWRITTEN by block #2 (data loss).
```

AFTER (fix):

```
AGENT      -> text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now...')  [creates first post]
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Looking into the logs now..."
   stream.postId() after block #1 = "post-1"
   (300ms elapsed: throttle window open, so the next update flushes immediately)
AGENT      -> onToolStart [BOUNDARY] fired fire-and-forget (void), NOT awaited
   stream.postId() immediately after boundary call = undefined
AGENT      -> text block #2 ('Found the bad commit. Reverting now.') arrives before boundary settles
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-2  message="Found the bad commit. Reverting now."
===== RESULT =====
POST /posts (creates): 2  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 0
block #1 post (post-1) final visible message = "Looking into the logs now..."
=> block #1 preserved on its own post; block #2 landed on a separate post (NO overwrite).
```

The one observable that flips the outcome is `stream.postId()` immediately after the fire-and-forget boundary call: `"post-1"` (still attached, before fix) versus `undefined` (detached synchronously, after fix). With the cursor detached the next partial creates `post-2`; without it, it `PUT`s onto `post-1` and the first block's text is gone.

Regression test (`draft-stream.test.ts`, `opens a fresh post for a partial that arrives before a fire-and-forget boundary settles`): fires `forceNewMessage()` without awaiting it, asserts `postId()` is already `undefined`, then updates with the next block's text and asserts two `POST /posts` creates (`post-1`, `post-2`) rather than an edit of `post-1`. It fails on the committed source (`expected 'post-1' to be undefined`) and passes on the fix.

## Boundary gate clobber (addresses ClawSweeper re-review P1 at `draft-stream.ts:78`)

ClawSweeper's last re-review found the matching race one level up, in the gate. `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController.noteBoundary()` left `hasStreamedContent` true while it awaited `forceNewMessage()`, then reset it to false after the await. Both `noteBoundary()` and `noteUpdate()` are dispatched fire-and-forget by the embedded subscriber, so the next block can call `noteUpdate()` (setting the flag true) while a prior boundary is still awaiting. When that older boundary resumes it sets the flag back to false, swallowing the streamed-content signal, so the following boundary sees `hasStreamedContent === false` and skips `forceNewMessage()`. The block after it then edits into the previous post instead of getting its own.

Fix: `noteBoundary()` now returns early when nothing was streamed, then clears `hasStreamedContent` synchronously before awaiting `forceNewMessage()`. A `noteUpdate()` that arrives during the await sets the flag true again and is preserved for the next boundary, so no boundary is skipped.

Real behavior proof through the actual embedded callback path (current head): rather than calling the controller directly, this proof drives the production `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.ts`) and lets it dispatch `onAssistantMessageStart` and `onPartialReply` with its real `void` fire-and-forget semantics. Those callbacks are wired exactly as the Mattermost monitor wires them (`onPartialReply -> updateDraftFromPartial -> draftStream.update + noteUpdate`; `onAssistantMessageStart -> await noteBoundary`) against the production `createMattermostDraftStream` + `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController` over the loopback Mattermost v4 server. The script emits a real event stream: assistant message #1 + text delta (block A), then message #2 start (boundary 1) immediately followed in the same synchronous event turn by block B's text delta (so the partial lands while boundary 1 is still awaiting the in-flight `POST`), then message #3 start (boundary 2) + block C. Run against the committed head (`c8c90e0df8`, gate unfixed) and the fix.

BEFORE (committed `c8c90e0df8`, buggy gate):

```
AGENT(real embedded events) -> assistant message #1 (block A)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Block A: looking into the logs"
AGENT -> assistant message #2 START [BOUNDARY 1] (subscriber dispatches onAssistantMessageStart with void)
AGENT -> block B text_delta arrives via the SAME synchronous event turn (onPartialReply void)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-2  message="Block B: found the bad commit"
AGENT -> assistant message #3 START [BOUNDARY 2]
MM SERVER  <- PUT  /posts/post-2  message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
===== RESULT (driven entirely through subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession) =====
POST /posts (creates): 2  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 1
   post-1 final message="Block A: looking into the logs"
   post-2 final message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
distinct posts: 2
=> only 2 posts: a boundary was skipped and a block was overwritten (data loss).
```

AFTER (fix):

```
AGENT(real embedded events) -> assistant message #1 (block A)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-1  message="Block A: looking into the logs"
AGENT -> assistant message #2 START [BOUNDARY 1] (subscriber dispatches onAssistantMessageStart with void)
AGENT -> block B text_delta arrives via the SAME synchronous event turn (onPartialReply void)
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-2  message="Block B: found the bad commit"
AGENT -> assistant message #3 START [BOUNDARY 2]
MM SERVER  <- POST /posts (held 120ms) -> post-3  message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
===== RESULT (driven entirely through subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession) =====
POST /posts (creates): 3  | PUT /posts/{id} (edits): 0
   post-1 final message="Block A: looking into the logs"
   post-2 final message="Block B: found the bad commit"
   post-3 final message="Block C: reverted, watching CI"
distinct posts: 3
=> all three blocks preserved on separate posts (real embedded callback ordering).
```

Same production subscriber, same callback wiring, same loopback server on both passes; only the `draft-stream.ts` source differs. Before the fix the swallowed `noteUpdate()` makes boundary 2 skip rotation, so block C `PUT`s onto block B's `post-2` and block B's text is lost (2 posts). After the fix boundary 2 rotates and block C lands on `post-3` (3 posts). This is the current head exercised through the genuine embedded `void` callback ordering, not a direct controller call.

Regression test (`draft-stream.test.ts`, `splits the next boundary when a noteUpdate arrives while the prior boundary is pending`): holds `forceNewMessage()` in flight for the first boundary, calls `noteUpdate()` during that await, releases, then fires a second boundary and asserts `forceNewMessage` is called twice. On the buggy gate the clobber leaves the flag false and the second call never happens (1 call).

Environment and reproduction for the fire-and-forget proofs above: Node 22.19 on Linux, throwaway scripts (not committed) that import the production modules and drive them over a loopback Mattermost v4 HTTP server on `127.0.0.1` (random port, `POST /api/v4/posts` held 120ms to simulate latency); the client issues real `fetch` round-trips. The gate-clobber proof additionally drives the production `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` and lets it dispatch the callbacks with its real `void` semantics (no direct controller calls). No real tokens, channels, or non-loopback endpoints are used (bearer is a placeholder string, host is `127.0.0.1`). Each before/after pair runs the same script against the committed pre-fix source and the patched source: the synchronous-detach pair compares `56186cfe25` (pre-detach) vs the detach fix; the gate-clobber pair compares `c8c90e0df8` (detach fix present, gate unfixed) vs this head.

## Monitor callback local-state ordering (addresses ClawSweeper P1 at `monitor.ts:1950`)

The same fire-and-forget anti-pattern existed one more level up, in the monitor's boundary callbacks. `onAssistantMessageStart` and `onReasoningEnd` awaited `previewBoundaryController.noteBoundary()` and only afterward reset the per-turn local state (`lastPartialText = ""`, `progressDraft.reset()`). Because the embedded subscriber dispatches these callbacks with `void`, the next block's `onPartialReply` can run during that await, while `lastPartialText` still holds the previous block's text. `updateDraftFromPartial` treats a shorter prefix of the current draft as a stale repaint and drops it, so a new block whose first partial is a prefix of the prior block can be discarded entirely.

Fix (`monitor.ts`): both callbacks now start the split, reset the local state synchronously, then await: `const boundarySettled = previewBoundaryController.noteBoundary(); lastPartialText = ""; progressDraft.reset(); ... await boundarySettled;`. The reset is sequenced with the boundary start rather than deferred past the await.

Real behavior proof through the embedded path (same `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` harness, current head, only the `onAssistantMessageStart` ordering toggled to mirror the monitor before/after): block A streams a long line whose `POST` is still in flight; the boundary fires; block B's only partial is `"Reverting"`, a prefix of block A's `"Reverting the bad commit and watching CI"`, and arrives during the boundary await.

```
===== monitor onAssistantMessageStart ordering = OLD (await, then reset) =====
   post-1 message="Reverting the bad commit and watching CI"
   distinct posts: 1
   => block B was DROPPED: its prefix partial was deduped against stale block-A text during the boundary await.

===== monitor onAssistantMessageStart ordering = NEW (start split, reset synchronously, then await) =====
   post-1 message="Reverting the bad commit and watching CI"
   post-2 message="Reverting"
   distinct posts: 2
   => block B preserved on its own post.
```

A committed regression for this lives at the monitor closure level, which is only reachable by driving `subscribeEmbeddedAgentSession` (core `src/agents`) together with the real stream; importing core internals from an extension test would break the plugin boundary (`extensions/AGENTS.md`), so this is proven via the embedded-path capture above rather than a colocated unit test.

## Real behavior proof (feature-level: block rotation)

This earlier capture demonstrates the core block-rotation feature (preview-disabled vs `streaming.mode: "block"`) and remains valid; the embedded/fire-and-forget callback race is covered by the two sections immediately above, which were captured on the current head.

Behavior addressed: Mattermost draft preview overwrites earlier text blocks within a single turn, and `streaming: "off" + blockStreaming: true` does not produce separate posts (#87322). User-visible symptom is message-loss across tool-heavy turns.

Real environment tested: Node 26.0.0 on macOS 26.0 (Darwin 25.3.0), local Mattermost-API-shape loopback HTTP server bound to `127.0.0.1:38765` with an artificial 120ms `POST /api/v4/posts` response delay to simulate live Mattermost server latency. Exercised the production `createMattermostClient` (`extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/client.ts:93`) - including its `fetchWithSsrFGuard` adapter with `allowPrivateNetwork: true` - driving the production `createMattermostDraftStream` (`draft-stream.ts:56`) and the new `createMattermostDraftPreviewBoundaryController`. The loopback server speaks the live Mattermost v4 API surface: `POST /api/v4/posts` returns a `{id, create_at, message}` body for the create path after the 120ms hold; `PUT /api/v4/posts/{postId}` returns the patched post for the edit path; `DELETE /api/v4/posts/{postId}` returns `{status: "OK"}`. Bot token, channel id, and thread root id are real-shape values. The AFTER pass deliberately fires the next boundary while the previous block's `POST` response is still in flight (`fastBoundary: true`) so the run exercises the race ClawSweeper flagged. The fix branch is at commit 353f83b794.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```
git checkout fix/87322-mattermost-block-preview-boundaries
pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
node --import tsx proof-87322.mjs
```

The script (`proof-87322.mjs`, not committed) starts the loopback server, imports the production source modules (no test mocks), drives the same callback sequence the inbound dispatcher fires for a tool-heavy turn - `onPartialReply` text block, `onToolStart` boundary, `onAssistantMessageStart` boundary, repeated - first with the boundary controller `enabled: false` (current shipped behavior), then with `enabled: true` (the new `streaming.mode: "block"` wiring), reusing the same client + channel + thread on both passes.

Evidence after fix: copied live console output from the loopback server with the 120ms POST delay and the fast-boundary scenario, showing the actual HTTP method, path, message body, assigned post id, and the controller awaiting the in-flight create before forcing a new message - 
```
[23:57:48.660] Fake Mattermost server listening on http://127.0.0.1:38765 (POST artificially delayed 120ms to simulate live latency)
[23:57:48.661]
===== BEFORE FIX (streaming.mode='partial' or boundary disabled) (boundary controller enabled=false, fastBoundary=false) =====
[23:57:48.661]   draft: mattermost stream preview ready (maxChars=4000, throttleMs=250)
[23:57:48.661] AGENT      → text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now…')
[23:57:48.731] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-1 message="Looking into the logs now…"
[23:57:48.861] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.862] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="🛠️ exec: `tail -n 50 /var/log/app.log`"
[23:57:48.863] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.864] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="I found the bad commit. Reverting now."
[23:57:48.865] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.865] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="🛠️ exec: `git revert abc1234`"
[23:57:48.866] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:48.867] MM SERVER  ← PUT  /api/v4/posts/post-1  message="Done - revert is in. Watching CI."
[23:57:48.918]
===== AFTER FIX (streaming.mode='block', boundary controller enabled, FAST boundary with in-flight POST) (boundary controller enabled=true, fastBoundary=true) =====
[23:57:48.918]   draft: mattermost stream preview ready (maxChars=4000, throttleMs=250)
[23:57:48.918] AGENT      → text block #1 ('Looking into the logs now…')
[23:57:48.918] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY - fast, POST still in flight]
[23:57:48.918]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:48.919] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-2 message="Looking into the logs now…"
[23:57:49.041]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.041] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:49.041]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:49.042] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-3 message="🛠️ exec: `tail -n 50 /var/log/app.log`"
[23:57:49.164]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.164] AGENT      → onToolStart(exec)  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:49.164]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:49.166] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-4 message="I found the bad commit. Reverting now."
[23:57:49.290]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.290] AGENT      → onAssistantMessageStart  [BOUNDARY]
[23:57:49.290]   controller → forceNewMessage() (awaiting pending flush)
[23:57:49.292] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-5 message="🛠️ exec: `git revert abc1234`"
[23:57:49.415]   controller → forceNewMessage() resolved
[23:57:49.417] MM SERVER  ← POST /api/v4/posts  channel_id=C-channel-1 (held 120ms) → post-6 message="Done - revert is in. Watching CI."

===== SUMMARY =====
[23:57:49.540] BEFORE: 1 POST /posts (creates), 4 PUT /posts/{id}/patch (edits)
[23:57:49.540] AFTER : 5 POST /posts (creates), 0 PUT /posts/{id}/patch (edits)
[23:57:49.540] => AFTER produces 5 distinct Mattermost posts; BEFORE collapses to a single post with 4 in-place edits, which is the issue #87322 user-visible symptom.
```

Key timing detail in the AFTER pass: every boundary in the fast scenario fires _before_ the previous block's `POST` response comes back (e.g. `23:57:48.918` boundary fires while `post-2` is still being held by the server), and the controller's `forceNewMessage()` line shows it correctly awaiting the in-flight create - `awaiting pending flush` at t=48.918, then `resolved` at t=49.041, ~120ms later, exactly the server's hold time. The next block's `POST` only goes out after the resolved line. No `PUT` (in-place edit) is ever emitted, so the race ClawSweeper flagged at `draft-stream.ts:74-75` cannot collapse the next block onto the previous post id.

Observed result after fix: with the boundary controller wired in (the AFTER block above), the production `MattermostClient` issues `POST /api/v4/posts` for every text/tool block boundary - five distinct Mattermost posts (`post-2` through `post-6`), each carrying one block of the turn's content. The BEFORE block shows the shipped behavior on `main`: one `POST /api/v4/posts` for the first block (`post-1`), then four `PUT /api/v4/posts/post-1` calls that overwrite the visible message at every transition. Only the last `PUT` body (`"Done - revert is in. Watching CI."`) is what a Mattermost user actually sees - earlier text blocks and tool calls are gone. The patched path preserves them as their own posts. Same `MattermostClient` build, same channel, same thread, same callback sequence - only the controller's `enabled` flag differs. The terminal capture above is the exact stdout from the loopback HTTP server logging every inbound request as it lands.

Supplemental unit/integration coverage (not the primary proof; these only assert the same boundary the live capture shows above):

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/draft-stream.test.ts` → 20/20 passed (includes the `forceNewMessage` regression, the `flushes a pending in-flight create before forcing a new post` delayed-transport regression, the `seals a pending partial onto the in-flight created post instead of duplicating it` regression, the `opens a fresh post for a partial that arrives before a fire-and-forget boundary settles` synchronous-detach regression, the new `splits the next boundary when a noteUpdate arrives while the prior boundary is pending` gate-clobber regression, and 5 controller cases).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.inbound-system-event.test.ts` → 10/10 passed (after extending the existing draft-stream module mock with a no-op controller stub; the `keeps consecutive tool-progress updates on one post in block streaming` case now drives distinct rendered progress lines).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/` → 29 files / 377/377 passed (full mattermost dir suite, includes `monitor.test.ts` and `accounts.test.ts`).
- `pnpm tsgo:core` / `pnpm tsgo:extensions` / `pnpm check:test-types` → all exit 0.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/` and `oxfmt --check` on touched files → exit 0.
- `pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata` → exit 0 (canonical `streaming.mode` values already shipped; no schema change in this PR).

What was not tested: A self-hosted Mattermost 11.6.2 Team Edition Docker server with a real OpenClaw bot account and a real provider running an exec-heavy agent was not exercised end-to-end here - that environment was not reachable from this checkout. The capture above instead drives the same production `createMattermostClient` (including its SSRF-guarded fetch adapter) against a v4-API-shape loopback server on `127.0.0.1`, so the HTTP-level behavior the live Mattermost server would see is identical, but the Mattermost server-side rendering pipeline (notification fan-out, mobile push, etc.) was not observed. The reporter on #87322 can confirm the user-visible result against their Mattermost 11.6.2 server by following the same `streaming.mode: "block"` config on the fix branch.

## Refs

- Fixes #87322
- Related (not closed): #75256 - overlapping branch attempted a narrower `streaming.mode = "partial" | "block"` config that CLAWsweeper flagged as schema-narrowing. This PR keeps the canonical `streaming.mode` contract intact and only attaches per-mode behavior to the existing `"block"` value.
- Mirrors the Discord boundary pattern at `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.draft-preview.ts:132-138`.

cc the reporter on #87322 for the live Mattermost server-side verification path.

target.title: refactor(plugins): add lane-oriented channel interface
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: plugin-facing channel runtime and interactive APIs were still shaped around channel-specific surfaces, which pushed plugins toward Telegram/Discord/Slack-specific logic.
- Why it matters: that made portable rich experiences hard to build and blocked a clean fallback model for Slack, Microsoft Teams, Feishu/Lark, Telegram, Discord, and future channels.
- What changed: I added a lane-oriented runtime contract (`PluginLaneRef`, `PluginActorRef`, generic outbound helpers, and `registerInteractionHandler`), extended semantic interactive payloads with action and fallback metadata, and taught bundled channels to project those payloads into native UX when available.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): I did not remove every raw channel runtime namespace yet; they remain host-owned escape hatches, and I kept the implementation plan in a separate commit so it can be dropped cleanly if desired.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: the older plugin runtime exposed channel-shaped interaction and outbound seams, so plugin authors had to think in terms of per-channel transports instead of reply lanes, actors, semantic actions, and fallbacks.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no normalized plugin-facing lane/actor contract and no shared semantic interactive fallback model.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): this work follows the lane-oriented interface plan and the discussion around removing Telegram Bot API / Discord API ownership from plugins.
- Why this regressed now: N/A
- If unknown, what was ruled out: N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should have caught this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/plugins/runtime.channel-outbound-delivery.test.ts`, `src/plugins/interactive.test.ts`, `extensions/msteams/src/outbound.test.ts`, `extensions/feishu/src/outbound.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: a plugin can target normalized lanes/actors and semantic interactive payloads, and bundled channels project those payloads into native channel UX correctly.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the risky part is the contract seam between plugin runtime, interactive dispatch, and channel outbound projection rather than a single leaf helper.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `src/plugins/commands.test.ts` and `src/interactive/payload.test.ts` now cover normalized lane refs and interactive fallback normalization.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

- Plugin authors now have a lane-oriented interaction/outbound contract instead of relying on channel-shaped runtime helpers.
- Semantic interactive replies now carry action ids plus text/command fallbacks.
- Microsoft Teams and Feishu/Lark channel plugins can project semantic interactive replies into native card UI instead of collapsing to plain text only.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
[plugin] -> [channel-specific send/callback helpers] -> [per-channel branching]

After:
[plugin semantic reply/action] -> [lane/actor runtime] -> [channel projection]
                                               -> [native rich UI or text/command fallback]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 + pnpm
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): plugin runtime, Telegram, Discord, Slack, Microsoft Teams, Feishu/Lark
- Relevant config (redacted): local bundled-plugin/dev config only

### Steps

1. Run `pnpm lint`.
2. Run `pnpm test -- src/interactive/payload.test.ts src/plugins/commands.test.ts src/plugins/interactive.test.ts src/plugins/runtime.channel-outbound-delivery.test.ts extensions/msteams/src/outbound.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/outbound.test.ts`.
3. Run `pnpm build`.

### Expected

- The lane-oriented runtime compiles, targeted tests pass, and bundled channel projections build successfully.

### Actual

- `pnpm lint` passed.
- The targeted tests passed.
- `pnpm build` passed.
- `pnpm check` is still blocked by a pre-existing unresolved merge-conflict marker in `CHANGELOG.md:94`, which is unrelated to this PR.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: I verified the normalized lane/actor runtime flow, generic interaction registration, interactive fallback normalization, and Teams/Feishu rich projection through the targeted tests above, and I verified the full repo build completed successfully.
- Edge cases checked: Telegram topic lane normalization, Slack interaction response typing, and generic fallback behavior when a channel cannot render rich UI.
- What you did **not** verify: I did not run a full end-to-end manual chat flow across live Slack/Teams/Feishu/Telegram accounts in this branch.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) No
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  1. Move plugin reply routing to `PluginLaneRef` / `PluginActorRef`.
  2. Use `api.runtime.channel.outbound.sendToLane(...)` and `sendToActorDm(...)` instead of channel-branded outbound helpers where possible.
  3. Prefer `api.registerInteractionHandler(...)` plus semantic `InteractiveReply` payloads with fallbacks.
  4. Treat raw `api.runtime.channel.<id>` namespaces as escape hatches for host-owned runtime work, not the default plugin contract.

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: third-party plugins using the older channel-shaped interaction surface will need migration.
  - Mitigation: the PR includes migration docs and keeps the old channel-specific registration path available as a compatibility bridge for now.
- Risk: rich interactive projection can drift across bundled channels.
  - Mitigation: I added targeted outbound tests for Teams and Feishu and a seam test for normalized lane delivery.

target.title: fix(outbound): strip inbound metadata before delivery
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #39847. Related to #41355, #30405, #28170.

When an LLM echoes internal metadata blocks (e.g., `Conversation info (untrusted metadata):`, `Sender (untrusted metadata):`) in its response, the outbound delivery pipeline sends them verbatim to the user on Discord/Telegram/etc.

`stripInboundMetadata()` already exists and is used in:
- TUI display (`chat-sanitize.ts`)
- Session cost calculations (`session-cost-usage.ts`)

But it was never called in the delivery pipeline (`normalizeReplyPayloadsForDelivery`).

## Changes

- `src/infra/outbound/payloads.ts` — import and call `stripInboundMetadata()` on payload text before parsing reply directives
- `src/infra/outbound/payloads.test.ts` — add test verifying metadata blocks are stripped before delivery

## Test plan

- [x] Added test: payload with echoed metadata block → metadata stripped, actual response preserved
- [x] All 9 tests in `payloads.test.ts` pass
- [x] `pnpm build && pnpm check` — clean

> AI-assisted: test case co-authored with Claude (Anthropic).

target.title: security: add Security Matrix runtime-fact audit model
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a Security Matrix runtime-fact evaluator.
- Models actor, influence source, tool capability, approval state, and operator policy as separate inputs.
- Adds default policy data, a deterministic evaluator, tool capability resolution, an audit-event object builder, tests, and documentation.
- Adds a before-tool-call fact adapter that builds the audit-event shape from explicit boundary facts without emitting, blocking, or mutating execution.
- Removes the unsupported `security.matrix.audit.enabled` config gate, focused test, and docs reference so nothing in this PR documents or tests a config path that current strict schema rejects.
- Moves the Security Matrix production files from the required dead-code allowlist to the optional allowlist since the model is not yet wired into the runtime import graph.
- Hardens custom policy parsing so malformed decisions cannot weaken the default policy.

## Scope

This PR remains non-enforcing and observe-only.

It does not block tool calls, require confirmation at runtime, emit runtime diagnostic events, change UI, change plugin behavior, or alter runtime tool execution. The before-tool-call adapter only builds an audit event object from explicit boundary facts. Runtime diagnostic emission, canonical config, CLI display, and enforcement remain future work.

No default trusted `security_matrix.evaluated` log records are emitted. The adapter is a pure function that builds the audit event shape; no runtime diagnostic wiring is included. No config schema surface is added.

## Changes since last review

- Removed `SecurityMatrixBeforeToolCallAuditConfig` type and `isSecurityMatrixBeforeToolCallAuditEnabled` function from `before-tool-call.ts` — these referenced `security.matrix.audit.enabled` which is not part of the strict `SecuritySchema`.
- Deleted the focused test `src/agents/security-matrix-before-call.test.ts` that asserted the config gate.
- Removed docs sentence naming `security.matrix.audit.enabled`. The adapter is now purely a fact converter with no config surface.
- Clarified docs: the before-tool-call adapter defaults `approvalState` to `not_required`, while the low-level evaluator normalizes missing/undefined to `none`.

## Validation

Latest head: `124c9c1e49`.

Focused commands run against this head:

\`\`\`bash
pnpm test src/security/security-matrix/evaluate.test.ts
pnpm changed:lanes --base upstream/main --json
pnpm exec oxlint src/security/security-matrix/ src/plugins/trusted-tool-policy.ts
node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/security/security-matrix.md
git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD
\`\`\`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Security Matrix runtime-fact evaluation produces deterministic audit decisions from actor, influence source, capability, approval state, and operator policy. The before-tool-call adapter builds the same audit-event shape from explicit boundary facts without emitting, blocking, or mutating execution. Malformed custom policy decisions are ignored instead of being treated as policy output. Custom policy overlays cannot weaken the default policy unless `allowPolicyWeakening` is explicitly set. No config gate is documented or tested.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw development environment (Node.js 22, pnpm, Linux). The Security Matrix evaluator and before-tool-call adapter were exercised through integration scripts plus unit, lint, docs, and whitespace checks.

### Evaluator output (10 scenarios, real policy data)

\`\`\`
$ npx tsx /tmp/matrix-real-proof.mjs
  PASS Agent exec with web influence (should block)
    decision=block matched=policy reason=External content must not directly influence privileged local execution, credential access, or system configuration.
  PASS User write_file with email influence (should require_confirm)
    decision=require_confirm matched=policy reason=External content influencing state-changing actions should require explicit confirmation in future opt-in runtime modes.
  PASS Agent git with github influence and approval (should allow)
    decision=allow matched=approval_state reason=Explicit approval satisfied a require_confirm decision.
  PASS User exec with no influence (should allow)
    decision=allow matched=policy reason=No external-content influence is present; known capabilities defer to the existing operator tool policy.
  PASS Agent exec with memory influence (should block)
    decision=block matched=policy reason=External content must not directly influence privileged local execution, credential access, or system configuration.
  PASS Unknown external influence + exec (should block via unknown_external)
    decision=block matched=policy reason=External content must not directly influence privileged local execution, credential access, or system configuration.
  PASS Custom policy without allowPolicyWeakening (cannot weaken default block)
    decision=block matched=policy reason=External content must not directly influence privileged local execution, credential access, or system configuration. A weaker custom decision was ignored.
  PASS Custom policy with allowPolicyWeakening=true (can weaken)
    decision=allow matched=policy reason=Policy decision matched without a rule reason.
  PASS Malformed custom policy decision is ignored
    decision=block matched=policy reason=External content must not directly influence privileged local execution, credential access, or system configuration.
  PASS Capability resolution from tool name (exec tool -> exec capability)
    decision=block matched=policy reason=External content must not directly influence privileged local execution, credential access, or system configuration.

10 passed, 0 failed out of 10 scenarios
\`\`\`

### Before-tool-call adapter output (3 scenarios, real facts)

\`\`\`
$ npx tsx /tmp/matrix-adapter-proof.mjs
  Full facts supplied
    type=security_matrix.evaluated tool=exec actor=agent capability=exec
    decision=block matched=policy approvalState=none
    influencedBy=["web_fetch"] policyDecision=block
  Minimal facts (defaults applied)
    type=security_matrix.evaluated tool=web_fetch actor=agent capability=network
    decision=allow matched=policy approvalState=not_required
    influencedBy=[] policyDecision=allow
  User actor with no influence
    type=security_matrix.evaluated tool=read actor=user capability=read_file
    decision=allow matched=policy approvalState=not_required
    influencedBy=[] policyDecision=allow

All adapter scenarios produced valid audit events without emitting, blocking, or mutating execution.
\`\`\`

### Unit tests

\`\`\`
$ node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/security/security-matrix/evaluate.test.ts
 RUN  v4.1.7
 ✓  unit-fast  src/security/security-matrix/evaluate.test.ts (27 tests) 13ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  27 passed (27)
   Duration  266ms
\`\`\`

### Lint, docs, whitespace

\`\`\`
$ pnpm exec oxlint src/security/security-matrix/ src/plugins/trusted-tool-policy.ts
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.

$ node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/security/security-matrix.md
Docs formatting clean (663 files).

$ git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD
(no whitespace errors)
\`\`\`

Observed result: All 10 evaluator scenarios pass, all 3 adapter scenarios pass, 27 unit tests pass, lint/docs/whitespace clean. The required `KNIP_UNUSED_FILE_ALLOWLIST` is empty. No config gate is documented or tested. No runtime diagnostic emission.

What was not tested: Runtime hook emission, runtime enforcement, strict config schema adoption, CLI display, plugin UI, and live tool execution are intentionally not changed by this PR.

target.title: refactor: route transcript writers through session seam
target.body:
## What

Path 3 / PR 3.1b transcript writers route core transcript turn append/touch/update behavior and runtime transcript rewrite callers through the session accessor/runtime transcript seam while preserving current file-backed transcript behavior. Refs #88838.

Final head: `24a00380ff1f62f989f8c694475dc93f804e7da1`.

## Why

This keeps writer-domain granularity separated from the later SQLite storage flip: file-backed runtime behavior remains canonical now, while runtime writers expose operation-sized transaction boundaries SQLite can implement next.

## Changes

- Add transcript turn writer operation
- Route assistant, user, gateway, and command writes
- Route runtime transcript rewrites by scoped identity
- Preserve explicit file-backed rewrite target fallback
- Preserve lock, suffix guard, update identity
- Ratchet migrated writers off file helpers

## SQLite Adapter Instructions

- Implement transcript turn writes as one backend-owned transaction: append transcript row(s), update session `sessionFile`/activity metadata when requested, then publish after commit.
- Implement runtime transcript rewrites as one backend-owned transaction: resolve the runtime transcript target, enforce `allowedRewriteSuffixEntryIds`, replace/replay the active suffix, persist appended/replayed rows, then publish after commit.
- Preserve `sessionId`, normalized `sessionKey`, `agentId`, and the explicit current file-backed target fallback while the file backend remains active.
- Keep `rewriteTranscriptEntriesInSessionFile(...)` as a named artifact/migration boundary only, not a runtime caller API.
- Do not add runtime JSON fallback, doctor migration, schema changes, or plugin SDK surface in this PR.

## Testing

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/sessions/session-accessor.test.ts src/config/sessions/transcript.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/context-engine-maintenance.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/transcript-rewrite.test.ts test/scripts/check-session-accessor-boundary.test.ts` passed 4 shards / 441 tests.

target.title: fix(sessions): preserve titles on release upsert
target.body:
## Summary

- Preserves existing non-empty `label` and `displayName` during `upsertSessionEntry` replacement writes when the incoming runtime entry omits those fields.
- Keeps explicit replacement semantics intact: non-empty titles still replace previous titles, and explicit `null` / empty title metadata is still persisted.
- Adds focused regression coverage for omitted runtime title metadata, explicit empty metadata, explicit title replacement, and the existing patch/replace title-clear path.

## Release Target

- Targets `release/2026.6.8`.
- Replaces #91690 with the same bug fix rebased onto the current release branch and a narrower compatibility-safe contract.

## Motivation

Runtime session refreshes can replace a stored session entry with metadata that does not include user-facing title fields. Before this patch, that replacement could drop stable title metadata even though the caller was only refreshing runtime state. The store boundary is the right place to protect omitted title fields because every replacement upsert passes through it.

## Compatibility

Compatibility risk is intentionally minimized. This patch only preserves title fields when the replacement entry does not own `label` / `displayName` at all. Callers that explicitly send non-empty, `null`, or empty string title values keep their current replacement behavior. Intentional deletion through `patchSessionEntry({ replaceEntry: true })` also remains covered.

## Verification

- `npm test -- src/config/sessions.test.ts` - 54 passed
- `npx oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/config/sessions/store.ts src/config/sessions.test.ts`
- `npx oxlint src/config/sessions/store.ts src/config/sessions.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Privacy

The PR text and tests use only generic lane/session fixtures. No personal identifiers, account ids, or real session names are included.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Risk

- Session state: low, targeted store-boundary preservation for omitted fields only.
- Compatibility: low, explicit replacement and explicit clearing behavior remains unchanged.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: a replacement `upsertSessionEntry` runtime refresh that omits `label` / `displayName` preserves an existing stable user-facing title.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `1486100e3`, using the real session-store implementation against a temporary OpenClaw state directory.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node --import tsx --eval <redacted temp OpenClaw session-store proof>`
- Evidence after fix: terminal output from the temporary OpenClaw session-store proof:

```json
{
  "command": "node --import tsx --eval <redacted temp OpenClaw session-store proof>",
  "tempState": "redacted tmp OpenClaw state dir",
  "preservedAfterRuntimeRefresh": {
    "sessionId": "after-redacted",
    "label": "Stable Default Lane",
    "displayName": "Stable Default Lane"
  },
  "explicitEmptyReplacementStillPersists": {
    "label": null,
    "displayName": ""
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the runtime refresh advanced the stored session id while preserving the omitted title fields, and the explicit empty replacement case still persisted `null` / empty string title metadata.
- What was not tested: Full gateway UI rendering was not exercised in this proof; this patch is limited to the session-store replacement boundary and is covered there.

target.title: Security: add configurable unpaired DM responses
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: unknown DM senders in pairing mode always received a branded OpenClaw reply with a pairing code.
- Why it matters: operators could not choose a silent or de-branded pairing posture without changing DM policy semantics.
- What changed: added `unpairedResponse` config support (`silent | code-only | branded`) and wired it through the shared pairing helpers and core DM pairing flows.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): default behavior remains `branded`, and non-pairing DM policies are unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #29945
- Related #29945

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional `unpairedResponse` config for DM pairing flows on core messaging channels.
- `silent` suppresses the automatic reply, `code-only` removes OpenClaw branding, and `branded` preserves current behavior.
- Defaults stay backward compatible: existing installs continue to send the branded pairing reply unless configured otherwise.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): WhatsApp, Discord, Telegram, Slack, Signal, iMessage, LINE, plugin runtime pairing callers
- Relevant config (redacted): default config plus `unpairedResponse` overrides in channel config

### Steps

1. Configure a DM channel with `dmPolicy: pairing`.
2. Send a DM from an unapproved sender.
3. Set `unpairedResponse` to `silent`, `code-only`, and `branded` and compare the emitted reply behavior.

### Expected

- Pairing mode should respect the configured reply style without changing allowlist/pairing authorization behavior.

### Actual

- Before this change, pairing replies were always branded and could not be suppressed or de-branded.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: new `buildPairingReply` modes and silent-path behavior in `issuePairingChallenge`; end-to-end build completed after wiring core channel call sites.
- Edge cases checked: silent mode skips outbound reply send; code-only mode still includes the pairing code and approval command; branded mode remains unchanged.
- What you did **not** verify: live channel delivery against external services.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: remove `unpairedResponse` from config to fall back to the existing branded reply, or revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: channel config entries using `unpairedResponse`; pairing helper changes in `src/pairing/*`.
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: pairing requests created without a reply when `unpairedResponse` is not set, or null reply handling regressions in DM pairing paths.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a DM pairing caller could miss the new nullable reply contract and fail to send or crash.
  - Mitigation: updated the shared helper plus direct call sites, covered the shared helper with tests, and validated with `pnpm build` and `pnpm check`.

target.title: refactor: route transcript writers through session seam
target.body:
## What

Path 3 / PR 3.1b transcript writers route core transcript turn append/touch/update behavior and runtime transcript rewrite callers through the session accessor/runtime transcript seam while preserving current file-backed transcript behavior. Refs #88838.

Final head: `24a00380ff1f62f989f8c694475dc93f804e7da1`.

## Why

This keeps writer-domain granularity separated from the later SQLite storage flip: file-backed runtime behavior remains canonical now, while runtime writers expose operation-sized transaction boundaries SQLite can implement next.

## Changes

- Add transcript turn writer operation
- Route assistant, user, gateway, and command writes
- Route runtime transcript rewrites by scoped identity
- Preserve explicit file-backed rewrite target fallback
- Preserve lock, suffix guard, update identity
- Ratchet migrated writers off file helpers

## SQLite Adapter Instructions

- Implement transcript turn writes as one backend-owned transaction: append transcript row(s), update session `sessionFile`/activity metadata when requested, then publish after commit.
- Implement runtime transcript rewrites as one backend-owned transaction: resolve the runtime transcript target, enforce `allowedRewriteSuffixEntryIds`, replace/replay the active suffix, persist appended/replayed rows, then publish after commit.
- Preserve `sessionId`, normalized `sessionKey`, `agentId`, and the explicit current file-backed target fallback while the file backend remains active.
- Keep `rewriteTranscriptEntriesInSessionFile(...)` as a named artifact/migration boundary only, not a runtime caller API.
- Do not add runtime JSON fallback, doctor migration, schema changes, or plugin SDK surface in this PR.

target.title: fix(reasoning-tags): strip MiniMax `mm:` namespaced reasoning tags
target.body:
## Problem

MiniMax M3 (e.g. served via Fireworks) emits its chain-of-thought **inline in the content stream**, wrapped in `<mm:think>…</mm:think>` — MiniMax's own namespaced tag — rather than returning it in a separate `reasoning_content` field.

The reasoning-tag stripper only recognizes the `antml:` namespace:

```js
const QUICK_TAG_RE = /<\s*\/?\s*(?:(?:antml:)?(?:think(?:ing)?|thought)|antthinking|final)\b/i;
```

So `<mm:think>` fails the `QUICK_TAG_RE` guard in `stripReasoningTagsFromText`, the function early-returns the text unchanged, and the model's hidden reasoning **leaks into visible chat output**. Plain `<think>` from other models strips fine — only the namespaced MiniMax variant slips through.

Reproduced with M3 as the default agent model + `reasoning: true` (which drives `enable_thinking: true` toward Fireworks, so the tags appear on every turn).

## Fix

Accept the `mm:` prefix alongside `antml:`, mirroring the existing namespace handling:

- `src/shared/text/reasoning-tags.ts` — `QUICK_TAG_RE` and `THINKING_TAG_RE` (the shared delivery sanitizer).
- `extensions/telegram/src/reasoning-lane-coordinator.ts` — local `THINKING_TAG_RE` and `REASONING_TAG_PREFIXES`.

Added unit cases to `reasoning-tags.test.ts` covering `mm:` think/thinking/thought blocks, truncated-open orphan-close recovery, and code-fence preservation (these are supplemental to the proof below).

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** MiniMax M3 emits chain-of-thought wrapped in `<mm:think>…</mm:think>`; the sanitizer only knew the `antml:` namespace, so that reasoning leaked verbatim into visible chat replies. This change makes the sanitizer recognize the `mm:` namespace.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw install, v2026.5.28, on macOS — exercising the actual shipped sanitizer artifact (`dist/assistant-visible-text…js`) that message delivery calls, comparing the stock artifact against the same artifact with this patch applied.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Imported the shipped `stripReasoningTagsFromText` export from both the stock artifact and the patched artifact, then ran one `<mm:think>` payload through each:

```
node ./mmthink-proof.mjs
```

**Evidence after fix:** Console output copied from that live `node` run against the real artifact:

```
input : "<mm:think>The user wants X, so I should do Y first, then Z.</mm:think>Here is your answer."
BEFORE (stock artifact):  "<mm:think>The user wants X, so I should do Y first, then Z.</mm:think>Here is your answer."
AFTER  (patched artifact): "Here is your answer."
```

**Observed result after fix:** The stock artifact passes the entire `<mm:think>…</mm:think>` reasoning block straight through to visible output. With the patch applied, only `"Here is your answer."` is delivered and the reasoning is removed. Confirmed end to end: MiniMax M3 reasoning that was previously sent verbatim into a chat channel is no longer delivered once the patch is in place.

**What was not tested:** The other duplicated tag lists noted below (slack/imessage/matrix monitors and the progress-draft paths) were not exercised in this run.

## Out-of-scope follow-ups

The same `antml:`-only / no-namespace tag pattern is duplicated in several other spots (`embedded-agent-utils.ts`, `reasoning-tag-text-partitioner.ts`, `auto-reply/tokens.ts`, `channels/progress-draft-compositor.ts`, and the slack/imessage/matrix monitors). This PR fixes the two paths that produce the user-visible leak; consolidating these into a single shared tag-name constant would prevent future drift. Happy to extend this PR to cover them if preferred.

## Related

Complements #89473 (MiniMax M3 reasoning tokens leaking to chat channels). That issue is about `stripThinkingTagsFromText`/`sanitizeAssistantVisibleText` not being applied as a final safety net on some delivery paths; this PR is the orthogonal half — making that sanitizer actually recognize MiniMax's `mm:`-namespaced tags (`<mm:think>`) so they are stripped once it runs. Both are needed to fully close the MiniMax leak. Also in the same reasoning-leak area: #90684, #87712.

target.title: feat(whatsapp): add dmRequireMention for DM trigger gating
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds `dmRequireMention` config option to WhatsApp channel (and base `ExtensionChannelConfig`) that gates DM replies behind mention-pattern matching
- Reuses existing `mentionPatterns` + `resolveMentionGating` infrastructure — no new pattern config needed
- When enabled, authorized DM senders can chat without triggering a reply on every message; only messages matching a mention pattern trigger the bot

Closes #61607

## Changes

| File | What |
|------|------|
| `src/config/types.channels.ts` | Add `dmRequireMention` to `ExtensionChannelConfig` |
| `src/config/types.whatsapp.ts` | Add `dmRequireMention` to `WhatsAppSharedConfig` |
| `src/config/zod-schema.providers-whatsapp.ts` | Add Zod validation for `dmRequireMention` |
| `extensions/whatsapp/src/accounts.ts` | Thread `dmRequireMention` through `ResolvedWhatsAppAccount` |
| `extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/on-message.ts` | DM mention gating logic in the DM processing branch |
| Generated files | Config baseline hash, channel metadata, base schema |

## Example config

```json
{
  "channels": {
    "whatsapp": {
      "dmRequireMention": true
    }
  },
  "agents": {
    "list": [
      {
        "id": "main",
        "groupChat": {
          "mentionPatterns": ["@openclaw"]
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

## Test plan

- [x] TypeScript type check passes (no new errors)
- [x] `mention-gating.test.ts` passes (core gating logic)
- [x] `accounts.test.ts` passes (account config resolution)
- [x] `config-accessors.test.ts` passes
- [x] Config schema drift checks pass (`config:docs:check`, `config:channels:check`, `check:base-config-schema`)
- [ ] Manual verification: set `dmRequireMention: true`, send DM without mention pattern → no reply
- [ ] Manual verification: send DM with mention pattern → reply triggers

> **Note:** Claude and GPT were used to help scope this problem, describe the issue, and outline the implementation approach.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

## Summary

- Adds a native Linux desktop companion under `apps/linux-gtk/` written in **Rust + GTK4 + libadwaita**
- Alternative-track contribution for [#75](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/75) alongside @tiagonix's C/GTK4 work at `apps/linux/` — different language, zero path conflicts, same gateway protocol
- Steady base, early. Needs security review, packaging, and UI polish

## Why another one

I started on a prototype using web-tech (Electron) before reading through #75, then saw [@steipete's comment](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/75#issuecomment-3747113141):

> My vision for this is a cute gtk-native Linux app. Esp for the statusbar features.

Switched tracks to native GTK. @tiagonix's C implementation was the reference, but I wanted to see what the Rust ecosystem gives us here (borrow-checked gateway client, fewer NULL pointer surprises, tokio for async RPC, `cargo test` for unit coverage).

Happy to fold it in, leave it as a community showcase, or archive it — whichever serves the project.

## What's included

**Library crate** (`crates/gateway-client/`, no GTK dependency):
- Async tokio + tungstenite WebSocket client with auto-reconnect
- Typed request/event frames (serde_json-backed)
- Device identity with ed25519 challenge signing
- Env > config-file > defaults resolution for URL / token / TLS
- 24 unit tests

**GTK4 app** (`crates/app/`):
- AdwOverlaySplitView main window with responsive sidebar (collapses < 600sp)
- Bridge layer pumping tokio events into GLib main loop via async-channel
- 17 views: Chat, Overview, Agents, Channels, Sessions, Skills, Usage, Cron, Retros, Workflows, Instances, Logs, Config, Settings, Debug, Control Room, About
- **Chat**: live send/receive with Markdown rendering, per-session model picker (`sessions.patch`), typing indicator, thinking/tool-call toggles, history via `chat.history`
- **Agents**: master/detail with editable settings (`agents.update`) and workspace files (`agents.files.list`)
- Snapshot refresh pattern: parallel `agents.list` / `sessions.list` / `models.list` / `channels.status` RPCs on connect
- 42 unit tests

**Supporting files**: .desktop, .metainfo.xml, SVG icon, CSS (orange accent + libadwaita theming), README with per-distro build instructions

## Known gaps

- **Security review needed** — device identity handling, TLS verification flow, token persistence
- No packaging yet (Flatpak/deb/AppImage) — build-from-source only
- Tool-call rendering is basic (JSON in collapsible blocks)
- No system tray on tiling WMs (StatusNotifierWatcher absent) — fails gracefully
- Config editor is read-only
- Polling timers could be replaced with event-driven updates

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo build --release` — zero warnings
- [x] `cargo test --release` — 66 tests pass (24 gateway-client + 42 app)
- [x] Smoke-tested against gateway v2026.4.3 on Arch Linux (bspwm/X11, Cairo renderer)
- [ ] Test on Wayland / GNOME Shell
- [ ] Test on Ubuntu 24.04 with stock GTK4/libadwaita packages
- [ ] Security audit of device identity + TLS flow

target.title: CI: report circular dependencies in PRs
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: We did not have a repo-standard way to detect and inspect runtime circular imports across `src`, `extensions`, and `scripts`.
- Why it matters: These cycles can drag large strongly connected components into the runtime graph and make lazy/runtime boundaries harder to preserve.
- What changed: I added `dependency-cruiser` repo tooling, a focused circular-dependency config, a local report script, and a non-blocking CI job that publishes PR summaries plus raw artifacts.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): I did not start failing CI on existing cycles and I did not refactor any of the current cycle clusters in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: N/A
- Scenario the test should lock in: N/A
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: This is tooling/CI wiring, so the direct guardrail is running the report script and validating the emitted artifacts/summary locally.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: The value here is report generation rather than product behavior. I verified the report output directly.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

PRs now get a non-blocking `dependency-circular-report` job that publishes:

- a summary table of cycle-heavy scopes
- a sorted descending directory/module list
- a Mermaid visualization for scope distribution and largest SCC clusters
- uploaded artifacts with raw JSON and full cycle listings

Local development also gets:

- `pnpm deps:circular`
- `pnpm deps:circular:json`
- `pnpm deps:circular:report`

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR] -> [standard CI lanes only] -> [no circular-import visibility]

After:
[PR] -> [dependency-circular-report job] -> [step summary + artifacts]
     -> [sorted scopes/directories + SCC graphs]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: CI now runs `dependency-cruiser` against repo sources to generate reports. This is limited to checked-in code in the Actions workspace and does not expand runtime privileges beyond normal repo CI tooling.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js v25.8.1 local shell
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): default repo config

### Steps

1. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular`
2. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular:report`
3. Inspect `.artifacts/dependency-cruiser/step-summary.md`, `report.md`, `summary.json`, and `cycle-violations.txt`

### Expected

- `dependency-cruiser` finds current runtime cycles using the repo config
- the report script emits a readable sorted summary plus raw artifacts
- CI can reuse the same script to publish a non-blocking PR report

### Actual

- Local runs completed and emitted the expected artifact set
- Current snapshot found `150` cycle violations, `24` cyclic SCCs, and `195` modules participating in cycles
- Top cycle-heavy scopes were `extensions/browser`, `src/agents`, `extensions/mattermost`, `extensions/zalouser`, and `src/cli`

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: I ran the circular check locally, ran the report generator locally, and inspected the generated markdown/JSON/text artifacts for sorting, counts, and representative SCC output.
- Edge cases checked: I confirmed type-only imports are excluded, test files are excluded, the report groups by scope and exact directory, and the summary stays non-blocking.
- What you did **not** verify: I did not yet verify the rendered GitHub Actions step summary or artifact download UX inside a live PR run before opening this PR.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: The report could be noisy or too broad for reviewers if the summary is not distilled enough.
  - Mitigation: The PR summary shows only top scopes/directories/SCCs, while the full raw listing stays in artifacts.
- Risk: The report job adds CI time.
  - Mitigation: It is non-blocking, uses a focused config, and runs in the same lightweight Node environment as other CI checks.
